speaker: interviewer
text: That's your email. Date, October 27, 1996. Survivor name, Morris Venezia. Interviewer name, Carol Stulberg. City, Inglewood, California. Country, United States of America. Language, English with an H. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg, S-T-U-L-B-E-R-G. I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Morris Venezia on October 27, 1996 in Los Angeles, California. for Survivors of the Shoah of Visual History Foundation. Good morning, Mr. Venezia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Carol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please give us your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name is Morris, M-O-R-R-I-S, which means Moshe. Second name is Venezia, V-E-N-E-Z-- as a zebra-- I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was [? Maurice, ?] which is something like Moshe in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born in February 1921 in Thessaloniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Thessaloniki at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was?

speaker: interviewer
text: The country.

speaker: interviewee
text: The country Thessaloniki is the capital of Macedonia, Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, I'm 75 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the origin of your name, Venezia? Where does it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the way they explain it to me is from the Inquisition of Spain. At the time, many Jews were there, were living in Spain. After the Inquisition, they went all over Europe. And I think, my parents they stopped right in Italy in Venice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, your ancestors from Spain?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Spain to Venice, Italy. And from Italy, they went to Thessaloniki, Greece. And everybody calling them the Venezianos, Venezianos. Because they came from Venezia. And I think this is the only way they got the name Venezia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my parents was Isaac, my father. And my mother, they used to call her Dudune in Turkish, which means something beautiful. Because she was the only-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the only female in the family after four brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was here making name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Her name from one was Angel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any other names besides Dudune?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know. But I think was Daisy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother, one brother, and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names and their age differences?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister is Rachel, which is Ra-hel. The other one, which lives now in Italy, is Shlomo or Solomon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other girl was Marika. And the little one was Marta

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you the oldest? Where you were middle? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father occupation was barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother worked, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us, please, what was your life like as a boy Thessaloniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in my neighborhood almost was call them the Baron Hirsch, all the Jewish people living over there. All poor people, but with wonderful life. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a house did you live in? live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] We had our own house in the neighborhood, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it to me. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the house like? Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: To us, it was beautiful. Because we're born over there with two rooms, kitchen, living room, and a big lot in front of the house. Which later, we sold our house. And my father built up a two story house in that lot. So there is there. We were living very beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] were happy. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Jewish background like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Jewish life? Well, actually my grandfather, I remember, he was at that time around 60, 65 years old. He never was so religious at that time. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was living with them, my father, my grandfather, and my grandmother. And he used to wake me up every morning around 6:00 to take me to the temple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Which, that was until I became 13. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, I got me-- and I got a bar mitzvah. After that, I say, maybe six months, something like that, they sent me in Italy. Because my father died at the time. And I was the older. My mother was with four kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Italian consulate, because we were Italian citizens, even if you born, everybody born in Greece, we were Italian citizen. And they send me in Italy, in Milan, to a college.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, let's go back a little bit. And let's talk about your grandparents. What do you remember about them? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- their name, my grandfather name, was like my name. I took after him. It was more Morris. My grandmother, she-- they used to call her Dudune, too. Because she was beautiful [INAUDIBLE]. In Turkish, Dudune means something good. I don't know what it means, but something very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you living with grandparents at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they loved me so much. My father passed away. And I had his name, my grandfather name. And they took very good care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died in 1934. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he die of natural causes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember exactly what kind of [INAUDIBLE] he had. But I think it was a gallbladder, something. He had a big operation. And a couple of days after the operation, he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, till the second elementary, I was in a neighborhood, the Jewish school in [INAUDIBLE]. And after that, because I was Italian citizen, they sent me to an Italian school till third and fourth elementary school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that-- the name of the school was called [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the fifth-- elementary fifth, I went to a brand new school they build-- the Italians over there-- called an [NON-ENGLISH], which was not elementary. It was the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the elementary, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a high school or gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a high school or gymnasium? I don't remember that. And after that, I was till the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] class of the-- of the gymnasium or the high school. And after that, they sent me in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before they sent you away to Italy while you were still in Salonika, what-- what was-- what brought about the fact that you were an Italian citizen and not a Greek citizen, because you were born in Greece? Why were different? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What were the different, what, of Italian citizen? Was nothing different.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how-- how were you an Italian citizen and not a Greek citizen? Because you were born in Greece. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's not like the United States, anybody who-- who born in the United States, they get right away the United States citizenship. But in other countries, they don't get it like that. They still have their citizenship of their parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if I wanted some day to become a Greek citizen, it was when I was going to be 21 years old and to decide if I want to go to serve in the Greek army or to the Italian army. Which, this never came up. Because we didn't have a chance, because of the war. So in other words, your parents were Italian citizens--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before. So you became Italian citizen as well? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. All their kids, Italian citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you had your bar mitzvah, did you have to go take bar mitzvah training and go for cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Of course. That's why my grandfather was waking up. And I hated that. Because wintertime, too cold to wake up at 6 o'clock in the morning to take me to the temple. After, I got the bar mitzvah. And I learned everything, which I don't know nothing about it now. I forgot everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, something that I remember was that when I put the tefillin in my arm, this is what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what Jewish holidays were like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very religious. And we had all the Jewish holidays, especially Passover. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to read the Haggadah every year-- [INAUDIBLE] year-- every year. We were a family, very close family with too much love. [CRYING] [SOBBING] Excuse me, Carol. I get too-- too emotional when I remember that. [CRYING] That's why I didn't want-- I never wanted to testify, anyway. Excuse me. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends mostly Jewish? Or, did you have Greek friends as well? Your friends from school, were they all Jewish? Or, you have mixed friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was all mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you all get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice, very nice. No problem. The only thing when in class when we had the Christian hour, the Jewish people would go out. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel any anti-Semitism at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all over the world there's anti-Semitism. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To me, it's because they're jealous of Jewish people, nothing else. But with our friends, we didn't have such a thing. But even at that time, the old people in Greece, they had something against the Jewish people. Because they were not educated people at the time, but not now, anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you used to like to do as a boy? What kind of activities did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always something funny. Since I was a boy, I was doing all my own toys by myself. Because we didn't have no toys at that time. It's not like now. And I used to do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many things. Even, I had in my mind to do a bicycle, which I never did. Because it was too expensive at that time. And nobody had the bicycle. But I was doing-- I was enjoying all the time to do something with my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of toys did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for example, I was doing what they call the thing to shoot for the birds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slingshot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Slingshot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was doing-- I was doing a couple pieces of wood with the steps on it and to go on it and-- and walk with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean like stilts, so you would be very tall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right, exactly. And excuse my English, [INAUDIBLE]. Because it cannot be like my native language. I can explain myself in my native language. But I think I'm doing all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing just fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made many, many other toys. I can name you thousands of them, many, many others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make toys for your brothers and sisters, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was playing with them. I was playing with them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you and your brother? Were you very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a change to be very close with my brother. Because when I was 13, they send me in Italy, like I said before. And when I came back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a couple girlfriends, which I didn't want my brother-- he's two years younger than I am at that time. I had my own friends. And he had his own friends. And I never liked my brother to come with me to go with a girlfriends. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference between the boys in the family and the girls in the family? Were they treated differently, and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: No differently at all. We were very, very lovely family. We loved each other. Even some time for dinner, if somebody was missing of us, we had to wait for that missing to come and sit down at the table. If not, we won't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give us an example, let's say, of perhaps what kind of was meals your mother prepared for Passover, what special foods?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all kosher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before Passover, that week, they had to redo it all the kitchenwares. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And to clean all the house nobody [INAUDIBLE] in the house. Carry the matzohs in a special container. And they used to do over there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fish, the carp, at the time. Because I think it was at the time the carp was big enough and very, very delicious. And then they used to-- to do meatballs of spinach or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some other kinds, many, many, many other kinds. But was everything else Passover-- kosher. We were not kosher at home. But at that time, everything was kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything my mother cooked I still remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your bar mitzvah, you were sent to Italy. And I want to talk about that. Just before we talk about that, can you tell me what the rest of the Jewish population was like in Salonika at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said before, there were poor people, very poor people. Because they were living in their community, the Jewish community homes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they used to call them Baron Hirsch. They were worse than the ghetto after that. The Germans made a gate over there. From there they were [INAUDIBLE], because was right at the train station, that neighborhood. But they had everybody-- they used to have a very good time, especially when the girls were getting married, big parties. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the-- when the children was born, very good parties. Poor, but a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: A large what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Synagogue, temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Was a brand new, almost brand new. A very big one, that was in Baron Hirsch only. Because we had some other synagogues all over Thessaloniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how the area got the name Baron Hirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way I know is a kind of a Baron Hirsch. Name was a person, a rich person, called him Baron Hirsch. And because this Baron he helped so much for this neighborhood and for the Jewish people, they give the name that neighborhood Baron Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the synagogue look like? Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like any other synagogue, very beautiful synagogue with [INAUDIBLE] at the end of that and with a chair. So it really was a beautiful synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- it was all Sephardic synagogue that you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Sephardic only. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were 13 after your bar mitzvah, you told us before that your grandparents sent you to Milan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my grandparents. My grandparents, they send me the Italian consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? Why did the Italian consulate send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother was with five kids. And she didn't have the-- the-- she didn't have the-- the way how to support us. And the Italian consulate, which they help us very-- too much. Not only us, to many Jewish people I can tell you that. After when-- even when the Germans was in Thessaloniki, the Italian consulate was giving some false certificates to get them out from the ghetto, which it happened to many people that I know. So they sent me to Italy. They proposed to my mother to send me to Italy to study over there. Because I was the oldest of the family. And she couldn't afford to feed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went right to Milan to a private college, Milan, which I didn't have to pay nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were with about 300 people, 300 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young people with a church in it. And I was the only Jewish at the church. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a little behind from my class. And they priest every night, he would take me to his office to give me some lessons to be able to be in the same level with the other students. But I don't think this was a reason to help me out. Because after the priest was talking to me all the time about Christianity, Jesus, et cetera, which [INAUDIBLE] was too long-- too young. He couldn't convert me. He's, like, I'm telling all the time to everybody that I'm not religious. Because they made me that way. But I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school, did you speak Italian there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know to speak Italian? Did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Because I was since Saloniki I was going to school, in Italian school. And I learned Italian at school. When I went in Italy, then I learn it better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak when you were home between your parents and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We speaking the Ladino. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because my mother spoke a little bit Greek, my father a little better, my grandparents maybe not at all. They were speaking Turkey, because it was-- Macedonian was a [INAUDIBLE] from the Turkish empire at the time. So, but we spoke Greek and Ladino.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any other languages as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now I speak-- I hope, the English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I speak the Ladino, the Spanish which I learned here from the Mexican people, the-- the Castilian Spanish. That makes three. I still speak French, which I-- the most I forget it, Germans, which I don't want to know. In the camp, I used to speak. But I don't want to know. And I don't know nothing about it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And, of course, well, I can not say that I was speaking Poland or Russian, no, a little bit of those languages. But the best language that I know is Greek, Spanish, Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were in Milan going to this college, what were you studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was studying over there to be a mechanical engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the college. It was an internal college.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so was it like a dormitory that you were living in with other students or with a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Just a dormitory-- dormitory. And we were studying four hours a day, and four hours-- four hours theory and four hours in-- in the shop, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with your family while you were in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only by letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you go to this college in Milan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Milan, I was maybe around three years and a half. Maybe one more year I was in Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from 1934 when you arrived until about 1937 would you say, '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Around 1935-- my father dies in '34. '35, they send me to Italy. And then when they start their anti-Semitism in Italy, which was not like in Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1939, they send me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that period of time, were you aware of what was going on in Germany and Hitler's coming to power? Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah, not too much, really. Not too much. Because I was in college with my friends studying music and everything else. But I did not know nothing about politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you experience anti-Semitism while you were in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did my experience a what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anti-Semitism while you were in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know nothing about it, because I was internally in the college. I didn't know what was going on outside of the college. I didn't know nothing about it. And I was-- I didn't know how old I was at that time. I was maybe around 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you return back to Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: What month, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't remember what month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you returned to Greece? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very happy to be with my family again. But I couldn't help my mother or my family, because I didn't know nothing what to do, what to work with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [INAUDIBLE] was my brother, Shlomo, which was helping the family with a barbershop. He became a barber, too. That's the only income we had. And he was taking care of the family. I couldn't do nothing. I was go-- I would like very much to work over there to help my mother. But I couldn't find any work. Because after a year, they, the Germans, came in and it was everything upside down then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the Germans came in? When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1941, I guess. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1941, '40, '41, something like that. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember when they came in? What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Carol, this is another story. This is another story. When the Italian declare war against Greece, I remember one morning very early in the morning the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some policemens came to the house, a couple of policemens [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with a-- not with a uniform. And they took me away from my home, only me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because was one year that I came from Italy. And, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took me as enemy of Greece or to be sure. Because I was young. Maybe I was a kind of-- espionage, what do they call them? Spy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you right there. And we're going to change the tape and talk more about this.
